prompt: You code an O-ring as Y = 1 if damaged, 0 if not, and fit an ordinary straight line. Which statement is a real reason that model breaks?
options: The fitted line can predict probabilities above 1 and below 0, outside the legal range.
A straight line cannot have a negative slope.
Least squares cannot be computed when Y is an integer.
The predictor temperature must first be standardized or the line will not fit.
why: Section 13.1 lists three ways the straight line fails for a 0/1 response: the line is unbounded so it predicts probabilities outside [0,1], the variance pi(1-pi) is not constant, and the two-valued error cannot be normal. Logistic regression fixes all three by bending the line through the logit so the fitted probability always stays between 0 and 1.

prompt: Logistic regression has no formula, so glm iterates. Put one IRLS step in order.
steps: Start from the current coefficients beta
Form the linear predictor eta = X beta and probabilities pi = 1/(1+e^-eta)
Build the weights w = m pi (1-pi)
Build the working response z = eta + (Y - m pi)/w
Solve the weighted least squares fit of z on X for the new beta
Repeat until the coefficients stop moving
why: Theorem 13.5: each Newton step for the score equations is a weighted least squares fit of a working response z on X with weights w = m pi(1-pi), so logistic regression is iteratively reweighted least squares. Section 13.2 codes exactly this loop by hand and it matches glm to five decimals in six passes because the log-likelihood is concave with one peak.

prompt: These are the fitted O-ring damage probabilities at several temperatures. Tap the prediction you should trust the least.
why: Every flight in the data launched at 53 F or warmer, so the 31 F point sits 22 degrees below all experience. The S-curve is being extended into a region where no data can check its shape, so the tidy 0.99 hides real uncertainty (Section 13.1 and Try it 13.1). The warm-end predictions sit inside the data range and are far safer to quote.

prompt: On the log-odds scale, logistic regression is a straight line. Each point is a temperature and the observed log-odds of O-ring damage. Fit the line.
why: The logistic model sets logit(pi) = b0 + b1 X, so on the log-odds scale the relationship is a straight line (the right panel of the sigmoid figure in Section 13.1). The best fit here has slope near -0.22 and intercept near 12, echoing the glm fit logit = 11.66 - 0.2162 temp. Real logistic fitting weights each point by its information (Section 13.2), but the linear-in-log-odds shape is what you just traced.

prompt: Match each odds-ratio number from the fitted models to what it means.

pairs.meaning: each degree of warming cuts the damage odds by about 19 percent
cooling 10 degrees multiplies the damage odds nearly ninefold
each 10 glucose units raises the odds of a positive test by 50 percent
the predictor has no effect on the odds

why: Section 13.3: exponentiate a logistic coefficient (times the step size) to get an odds ratio, the constant factor by which the odds multiply per step, whatever the starting odds. An odds ratio of 1 is no effect, above 1 raises the odds, below 1 lowers them. These are the exact numbers from Example 13.3 for the O-ring and Pima fits.

prompt: The glucose coefficient is 0.0406, so e^0.0406 = 1.04. One sentence about it is wrong. Tap it.

sentences.text: Each extra glucose unit multiplies the odds of a positive test by about 1.04.
Each extra glucose unit raises the probability of a positive test by 4 percent.
That 1.04 is an odds ratio, a constant multiplier on the odds.
How much the probability moves depends on where you sit on the S-curve.

why: Section 13.3 and Try it 13.3: a logistic coefficient acts on the odds, not the probability. e^0.0406 = 1.04 multiplies the odds by 1.04 per unit, a 4 percent rise in the odds. The change in probability is not constant 4 percent: it is largest in the steep middle of the S-curve and tiny in the flat tails. Saying odds, not chance, avoids the most common error in applied logistic regression.

prompt: The Pima model at threshold 0.5 gives accuracy 0.77, sensitivity 0.57, specificity 0.88, AUC 0.84. One reading is wrong. Tap it.

sentences.text: It clears 88 percent of the true negatives but catches only 57 percent of the true positives.
An AUC of 0.84 means the model classifies 84 percent of cases correctly.
Lowering the threshold below 0.5 would catch more positives at the cost of more false alarms.
A single accuracy number can hide poor sensitivity, so report both.

why: Section 13.5, Example 13.5: AUC is not accuracy. AUC 0.84 is the probability that the model gives a random positive a higher predicted probability than a random negative, a ranking-quality measure across all thresholds; accuracy at 0.5 is a separate 0.77. Sensitivity 0.57 and specificity 0.88 come from the classification table, and moving the threshold trades one for the other.

prompt: A five-predictor logistic model has null deviance 974.75 and residual deviance 703.24. What is the whole-model likelihood-ratio test and its conclusion?
options: G2 = 271.5 on 5 df, far into the chi-square tail: reject that all slopes are zero.
G2 = 703.24 on 746 df: the model fits perfectly.
G2 = 974.75 on 1 df: the predictors add nothing.
You cannot test this without the individual standard errors.
why: Section 13.4: the whole-model likelihood-ratio statistic is the drop in deviance, G2 = null deviance minus residual deviance = 974.75 - 703.24 = 271.5, referred to a chi-square with degrees of freedom equal to the number of slopes set to zero, here 5. A chi-square with 5 df has mean 5, so 271.5 is enormous and we reject the null that all slopes are zero; the predictors together carry real information.
